sections.heading: Clinical Note
sections.section_value:
Chief Complaint
John presents with an abnormal lab finding, including a high blood sugar and a recent diagnosis of diabetes.
History of Present Illness
The patient reports experiencing significant stress and strain over the past couple of weeks, coinciding with increased visitor activity. He states that he did not monitor his sugar intake closely during this time, leading to elevated blood sugar levels. He was treated in the emergency room for the high blood sugar and has been placed on metformin. The patient reports experiencing anxiety, which he believes may have contributed to the stress. He denies recent seizures and reports that his last epilepsy evaluation was approximately three months ago. He reports no chest pain, shortness of breath, nausea, vomiting, or dizziness. He has experienced some trace pitting edema to his lower extremities, possibly related to recent fluid administration in the emergency room. He recently checked his blood sugar and it was 162.
Findings
Physical examination reveals clear lung sounds and a regular heart rhythm. There is trace pitting edema to the lower extremities. Vital signs are currently stable. A blood glucose level of 162 mg/dL was recorded. Hemoglobin A1c is 8%.
Assessment

sections.heading: Clinical Note
sections.section_value:
The patient's recent high blood sugar levels and elevated hemoglobin A1c indicate a new diagnosis of diabetes mellitus. His anxiety may be contributing to his overall stress and potentially impacting his blood sugar control.
Plan
I will order a repeat hemoglobin A1c to monitor his diabetes management. I will continue the patient on metformin 500 mg twice daily. I will schedule a follow-up appointment in four weeks to assess his response to treatment and adjust medication as needed. I will also recommend a referral to a therapist to address his anxiety.
